item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd just like to take a moment to respond to this and point out while some of that could happen, A) there's no way to be sure, society has undergone massive shifts before and B) that doesn't justify letting people die from hunger. That kind of thinking was why the potato blight in Ireland was so bad (that and good ole' fashioned racism), people were saying that the upswing in growth caused by the influx of food was bad so the counter balance of mass, preventable death was a thing that should exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see a lot of people in these comments missing something important.
The Foundation isn't (necessarily) containing this just on reflex.
If allowed to spread, this fungus would end civilization as we know it.

Let's imagine it becomes endemic, so that anyone who wasn't getting enough to eat could just turn green until the hard times passed.
Food production and distribution is estimated at 30-60% of total global economic activity, depending on who you ask and how you define terms.
Almost all of that is now unnecessary. Farmers, truckers, shipping, and most retail stores would no longer be economically viable and would quickly close. The knock-on effects would devastate the market for even those industries not directly related to food. Global unemployment on an unimaginable scale would be the least of the issues.

The closest real-world parallel is the dust bowl effect that lead directly to the Great Depression. The green plague would be orders of magnitude worse, and permanent.

Then you have the Malthusian issue. People are no longer starving, but they are still consuming other limited resources; water, vitamins, living space. So we still have scarcity, now spread even thinner with an even faster growing global population. And an infrastructure in tatters.

This leads directly to the type of endless wars that are the prelude to the Fallout games. Only instead of oil and uranium, the world powers squabble and bomb over sources of water to maintain their massive populations.

Military campaigns drag on for decades, because invading armies no longer have vulnerable supply lines that can be targeted to force a retreat. A fortified position can hold out for years without re-supply, so siege tactics are useless and every territory despute is now down to a bloody assault. Casualties sky-rocket, approaching 100% for the losing side in any skirmish.

The standard of living drops through the floor. To a squalor below what we currently consider subsistence living. Iodine, calcium, iron, and other vital trace dietary supplements become luxury goods available only the wealthy and powerful.

The number one cause of death is now violence; number 2 is a tie between choking goiters and scurvy.

Clichés survive to become cliché only by being true: the road to hell is paved with good intentions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this SCP because it's what truly highlights an extremely dark and disturbing aspect of the Foundation for me.

In the course of being so adamant in protecting the world from anomalies, the Foundation is happy to let people suffer and die from less anomalous sources.

There are a lot of scips and tales that try to indicate how "ebil" the Foundation is, but it almost always feels like a necessary evil - this, though?

This is just zealotry. And I love it.

All my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be changed to 2100 Calories (an uppercase C to indicate 2100 kilocalories, as opposed to 2100 calories, which would be 2.1 Calories).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: same here, I just love the reference!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
unlike the h

I was lightly enjoying the article, but that single line secured my upvote with the force of the sun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It wasn't that one but I do remember reading a very similar novel that cover the transformation and events leading to it. There definitely was some human stratification with not everyone able to afford the same level of cybernetic/genetic modifications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: not often you get scps that are good and have no easily visible downsides. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh, just saw this. Anyway, are logistics of getting food to soldiers really the main issue these days with armies? And you'd need quite a bit of vitamin water.

Even then, I doubt a single nation would be able to effectively hold onto it. So everyone would have it. And, even if you did end up with a revolution or two, that effect would probably be short term in the extreme (I really only see that happening if people are too starved and malnourished to revolt), and would still involve less suffering than starvation does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We need more Manna. It is possibly the most underused GOI on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also had this idea quite independently a few weeks ago (though I got my inspiration through the sea slugs that eat algae and then get chloroplasts in their skin) - but this does it nicely and in a cool way. I like the MCF.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be contained because it is anomalous, the foundation doesn't need another reason. That's one of the interesting things about this article. They destroyed a crop of wheat that would have lead to an outbreak, but would have also helped the local people a lot. So they deprived people of both supernatural and natural sustenance, doubling the damage. Why did they do it? Because it is anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…until someone realizes that it practically lets you field soldiers that can live on vitamin water and always have enough energy for action indefinitely, and every country goes to war for it.

At least Adam Richman can get rid of it easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part I like most in the article is that the fungus is not harmful or lethal per se, but could lead to large-scale political strife if it was ever released, hence the perfectly logical need to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's mildly interesting to me is that this seems like it could have a potential for causing abnormal weight gain if it spread in the wrong areas. Here in modern society, many people fail to get all of the proper minerals and vitamins without exceeding the caloric intake and thereby gaining weight. Now imagine if you already had a thousand or so calories being provided to you. Kind of like the commentary that Superman should probably lack the chiseled physique he often shows off, due to his body not needing to do much work to perform his usual feats of strength.

But, of course, that's one of the reasons why the Contain protocol is in place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The FDA's recomended daily levels (the amounts on food labels) is based on 2000 calories daily. But that number has gotten a lot of criticism. The average American consumes closer to 3000, athletes and the very overweight go much much higher than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The average human consumes 2000 calories daily, if I remember correctly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No.

Which is a very good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if a humani-terrorist organization deliberately infected food or water distribution networks with this stuff. Could the foundation contain the aftermath?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I think 2100 calories is too little for any difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: unsure if the formatting in the letter was intentional, but it was driving me insane, feel free to revert my last two edits if it was supposed to look messy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good.
One thing I'd perhaps emphasize more - photosynthesis implies increased water requirements. Might not be that good for those in water-poor countries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This should say something like "10,00 lux (for example, )", so people don't have to google "lux" to figure out how bright that is.

I disagree. Lux is the SI unit for brightness, which, along with the metric system, is the accepted scientific denotation. A scientific report wouldn't go on and explain how many apples are in 10 kilos. The SCP reports would be shown to scientists, and however crazy they might be, they would still have a basic idea on how illuminance works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1615 appears to be a species of fungus-like microorganisms, similar to mold, that is capable of forming colonies on organic material.

"Fungus" is an entire taxonomic kingdom (it goes Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species). The Foundation would at least say something like "SCP-1615 is a microorganism of the kingdom fungus, but of an unknown/new phylum".

is exposed to a level of light in excess of around 10,000 lux

This should say something like "10,00 lux (for example, ____)", so people don't have to google "lux" to figure out how bright that is.

however, should an SCP-1615-1 subject consume an amount of food in excess of around 1700 calories per day over the course of approximately seven days, the effects of SCP-1615 typically wear off immediately.

"Wear off immediately" doesn't work when compared to the time span of seven days; especially not with a time span of "approximately" seven days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I wouldn't want that in the article, it is a nice touch to have an Agent have their doubts about the Foundation's methods. I personally imagine that each site has an Ethics Advisor, but that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I understand removing the Ethics Committee reference, I sorta liked the whole angle with an Agent not being so keen on the Foundation's methods for containing this SCP. Perhaps there could be something on the level of a personal log that has the Agent venting about the crop burning without necessarily venting to the EC in particular? It'd be interesting to get a glimpse at how Foundation personnel might handle/cope with such grim "contain anomaly by all means necessary" situations as this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since nobody seems to like it, I've removed the Ethics Committee reference. It wasn't my intention to use it as a tacked-on name-check, but I can understand why it came off that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not even the same story as I was thinking of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was my thought too, I just thought it might need to be noted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Convergent evolution seems to be the most likely explanation. Kinda like how SCP-173 and the Weeping Angels are so similar, even though Steven Moffat doesn't seem to be aware of the SCP Foundation and whoever originally created SCP-173 did so before the first Weeping Angel episode aired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much, much better than what I came up with. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the concept was altogether a good one, though the circumstances surrounding the effects wearing off left the ending of the article proper a little meh.

Then I saw the MCF was involved, got very excited, and promptly realized that the inclusion of a GoI was the only thing taking my opinion of the article from indifferent to interested. I believe this is the very definition of tacked-on, though perhaps only because I wasn't won over by everything. Either way, the Ethics Committee mention is very tacked-on, a whole level on tacked-on-ness above the MCF. So I feel I must downvote for these reasons, but I don't think it's overall a poor concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good to see an SCP that's actually beneficial, for once. And the MCF is just an amazing idea for a GoI. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to be that guy, but I'm pretty sure I've seen this idea almost exactly, down to it being created as a philanthropic gesture, in in an SF story.

Not saying its copied since as far as I know it wasn't a huge print run or anything, or that it's a bad SCP, just thought folks should be aware of it.

Edit: Found the full text of that story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Ethics Committee mention seemed both unnecessary and thrown in there, and the use of a fungus is pretty stereotypical in human-affecting anomalies, but otherwise I liked it. It's interesting, and as somebody with no prior knowledge of the GoI, it didn't cling to the SCP to the point that I couldn't understand what was going on. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sure Rioghail is well aware of that.

Edit: derp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like when something ueful is good enough to be upvoted. The hook, the clinical tone. I can't see nothing bad here for me.

P.S: It's normal that is something is useful people will reject here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. I'm just a bit iffy on the use of the Ethics Committee as well, but it's good to see another MCF article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm iffy on a fungus causing the generation of chloroplasts and on the use of the Ethics Committee, but it was a good read nonetheless, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty sure I read this exact thing in an old sci-fi magazine when I was a teenager.

This is well-written, though, and i'm happy to see another MCF article, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice concept, good execution, and a well-done link to a GoI. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my favorite part is the third addendum. It sorta makes it hit home that these people, though well intentioned, are messing with stuff, and perhaps even more importantly, groups that they don't really understand, but they are learning. And they're realizing the lengths the Foundation will go to keep everything normal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda like the idea of a GOI that actually wants to try and find humanitarian applications for SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the "exposure to light in excess of 10,000 lux" was unnecessary, but other than that it was quite compelling. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A rewrite of the now-deleted SCP-1139 candidate, 'Weight Gain Manna' by Abodos, which got purged for creepy stuff related to fat fetishism. Abodos then gave me permission to re-write it, and this is what me and VAElynx came up with. Also credit to Smapti for SCP-1176, which inspired some parts of this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like SCPs that show the Foundation to be…not evil, in a grimdark sense, but just reactionary. As they would be. +1
